text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic appendectomy on a seven-year-old child. Two Allis forceps are applied on either side of the umbilicus to alleviate the crease. A 2mm stab wound is made, and an 8cm Veress needle is inserted into the abdominal wall, which measures 2cm in thickness. The needle is positioned at a 90-degree angle to the abdominal wall and a 45-degree angle to the patient's body. Two click sounds are heard, followed by irrigation and suction tests.

The surgeon performs the hanging drop test by placing a few drops of saline on the varus needle, which are easily sucked in, confirming proper placement. The insufflator is then attached, and the surgeon listens for a tympanic sound over the liver dullness area, indicating successful insufflation. The insufflator settings show a pressure of eight, with 700 ml of gas introduced, and the flow rate is adjusted according to the child's age.

The surgeon achieves an intra-abdominal pressure of eight millimeters of mercury using a pulsatile flow of gas, resulting in good abdominal distension. The Veress needle is carefully removed, and the incision is enlarged with a smiling incision over the inferior crease of the umbilicus. Mosquito forceps are used to dilate the obliterated vitelline intestinal duct, following a modified Scandinavian technique. A ten-millimeter trocar is inserted with a screwing motion, guarded to expose only five centimeters, and the cannula is attached. The telescope is introduced for visualization, with careful attention due to the limited space and thin abdominal wall in pediatric patients.

The surgeon uses a Striker mini alligator, a 1.8 mm percutaneous grasper, to hold the appendix without needing a cannula, minimizing scarring. The grasper is locked to stabilize the abdominal wall. The procedure involves three ports: one in the right hypochondrium, one in the right iliac fossa, and one in the left iliac fossa. The harmonic scalpel is introduced through the left iliac fossa port, with its silicon jaw positioned towards the appendix to prevent heat transfer during dissection of the appendicular artery.

The surgeon skeletonizes the cecum to access the base of the appendix, ensuring complete appendectomy. Peritoneal mobilization is performed to fully expose the base. Care is taken to avoid contact between the harmonic device and the ileum or cecum. The major appendix is dissected, and a Misra's knot is prepared outside the abdomen using a knot pusher. The striker mini alligator is introduced into the loop to secure the appendix tip.

The surgeon negotiates the loop and positions the tip of the knot pusher over the base of the appendix and the cecum junction. The Misra's knot is tied securely at the base of the appendix to prevent leaving a stump. The harmonic scalpel is then used to cut the remaining stump of the appendix, ensuring the mucosa is cauterized to prevent spillage. Care is taken to avoid contact with the cecum to prevent perforation.

The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the tip of the appendix and employs the rail rod technique to guide it into a 10mm cannula. The telescope is positioned to pull the appendix out, with the stump visible under vision. The telescope is gradually withdrawn while the cannula pushes the appendix out, ensuring the stump is fully extracted.
